description: Canonicalization and hashing test vectors. Implementations MUST reproduce these outputs exactly. Failure to reproduce is non-conformance with RFC-ACDP-0001.

vectors.name: Body canonicalization and SHA-256
vectors.description: Producer-supplied body fields with the RFC-ACDP-0001 §5.7 exclusion set already removed. Apply JCS, then SHA-256, hex-encode lowercase.

vectors.name: Lineage identifier derivation — vector 1
vectors.notes: Hash input is the UTF-8 bytes of the ctx_id string, including the 'acdp://' prefix.

vectors.name: JCS — number formatting
vectors.description: Trailing zeros after the decimal are dropped; integers carry no decimal point; negative zero canonicalizes to positive zero (RFC 8785 §3.2.2.3).
implementer_notes: Python implementations using stdlib `json.dumps(obj, sort_keys=True, separators=(',', ':'))` are NOT JCS-conformant on this vector: stdlib preserves -0.0 as '-0.0' rather than normalizing to '0'. Use the `jcs` library on PyPI (https://pypi.org/project/jcs/), or pre-process inputs to replace negative zero before serialization.
JavaScript: the `canonicalize` package on npm (https://www.npmjs.com/package/canonicalize) handles negative zero correctly.
If your implementation fails this vector, your JCS pipeline is non-conformant — fix it before claiming conformance with RFC-ACDP-0001.

vectors.name: JCS — array order preservation
vectors.description: JCS sorts object keys but preserves array element order.

vectors.name: JCS — empty / null distinction
vectors.description: Presence of "field": null produces a different hash than the absence of the field. Empty arrays and empty objects are preserved.
